notes.anchor: I wish you had invited me to dinner on the Ides of March: there would have been no leftovers.
notes.note: A grim joke: the Ides of March (15 March 44 BC) was the day Cassius and the others killed Caesar; had Cicero been at the "banquet" he would have made sure Antony too was dispatched, leaving no surviving Caesarians (the "leftovers") to trouble them now.
The consuls of 43 BC, A. Hirtius and C. Vibius Pansa, who took office on 1 January and led the war against Antony at Mutina.

notes.anchor: but the consulars are utterly disgraceful
notes.note: Consulars (former consuls) were the Senate's most senior members; Cicero complains that the rank-and-file and junior senators showed more courage against Antony than the ex-consuls did.
Me hercule, a common conversational oath "by Hercules," used for emphasis rather than any real invocation of the god.

L. Marcius Philippus and L. Calpurnius Piso were among the senatorial legati sent in early 43 BC to deliver an ultimatum to Antony at Mutina; the embassy failed and returned with Antony's counter-demands.

notes.anchor: in a cause that saves the state I have now turned popular
notes.note: Cicero, long distrustful of mob favor, notes the irony that his stand against Antony has now made him popularis (a favorite of the people) in a genuinely patriotic cause.

notes.anchor: he was so quick to succeed you
notes.note: Dolabella had been assigned Syria as his province, displacing Cassius almost as soon as Cassius arrived; critics mocked the haste, and opinion held he ought to be barred from the province.
